property_delta Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance Task 1/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a matched molecular pair for intrinsic clearance in human liver microsomes. Compound A: C[C@H](NC(=O)Cn1nnc2ccccc2c1=O)c1ccc(C(F)(F)F)cn1 Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance: 0.996 log CLint (µL/min/mg) Compound B: Cc1ccc([C@H](C)NC(=O)Cn2nnc3ccccc3c2=O)nc1 Compound B differs from A by the structural transformation: FC(F)(F)[*:1] → C[*:1] (Core/scaffold: C[C@H](NC(=O)Cn1nnc2ccccc2c1=O)c1ccc([*:1])cn1) Questions: 1. Predict the Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance of Compound B. 2. Will this transformation result in improved metabolic stability (lower clearance) or reduced metabolic stability (higher clearance)? 3. Explain the chemical/mechanistic reasoning behind your prediction. What structural or electronic features drive this change?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Analysis: CF3 → CH3 Transformation on Pyridine Ring --- ### 1. Predicted CLint for Compound B **log CLint ≈ 1.4–1.6 (µL/min/mg)**, corresponding to roughly 25–40 µL/min/mg — a 2.5- to 4-fold increase over Compound A (~10 µL/min/mg). --- ### 2. Direction of Change **Reduced metabolic stability (higher clearance).** The CF3→CH3 swap opens a new metabolic soft spot without compensating protection elsewhere. --- ### 3. Mechanistic Reasoning **CF3 is metabolically inert; CH3 is not.** The C–F bond (~130 kcal/mol) is refractory to CYP450 oxidation. CF3 groups are never hydroxylated in phase I metabolism — the position is completely blocked. When you replace it with CH3, you introduce an *aromatic methyl*, one of the most reliably oxidized motifs in drug metabolism: > ArCH3 → ArCH2OH (CYP1A2, 2C9, 3A4) → ArCOOH This benzylic hydroxylation is rapid and high-affinity for many CYPs. The resulting alcohol can undergo further phase II conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation), accelerating overall clearance. **Electronic effects compound the problem.** CF3 is strongly electron-withdrawing (−I dominant, significant −M through the ring). On the pyridine ring this creates a doubly electron-poor aromatic system — the CF3 cooperates with the pyridine nitrogen to reduce electron density at all ring carbons. This deactivates the ring toward electrophilic CYP-mediated aromatic hydroxylation at adjacent/ortho positions. CH3 is weakly electron-donating (hyperconjugation). While the pyridine N still dominates ring electronics, the methyl subtly increases electron density at the 3- and 5-positions (ortho/para to CH3), marginally sensitizing those positions toward aromatic oxidation as a secondary pathway on top of the benzylic route. **The chiral benzylic CH(CH3) is the shared metabolic liability.** Both compounds carry the α-methyl amine CH(CH3) adjacent to the pyridine, which is also susceptible to CYP-mediated oxidation. This background clearance is identical in both; Compound B adds the *additional* aromatic methyl pathway on top of it, explaining the multiplicative (not merely additive) effect on CLint. **Summary table:** | Feature | Compound A (CF3) | Compound B (CH3) | |---|---|---| | Soft spot at substituent | None — C–F inert | ArCH3 → benzylic oxidation | | Ring electronics | Strongly electron-poor | Moderately electron-poor | | Aromatic hydroxylation risk | Low | Slightly higher | | Primary CYPs implicated | CYP3A4/2C9 on backbone | CYP1A2/2C9/3A4 on CH3 | | log CLint (predicted) | 0.996 (measured) | ~1.4–1.6 | This CF3→CH3 exchange is a canonical example of why CF3 is so valued as a metabolic shield in medicinal chemistry — it simultaneously blocks the substituted position, withdraws electrons to deactivate the ring, and raises lipophilicity (clogP +0.4–0.5 vs CH3) without adding any oxidizable hydrogen.

Ground Truth

value_B: 0.9956
delta: 0.0000
direction: detrimental
transform: FC(F)(F)[*:1]>>C[*:1]
transform_population_mean_delta: 0.0462
transform_population_n_pairs: 65
transform_population_std: 0.4955

Evaluation Criteria

prediction_accuracy: Compare predicted value_B to actual (within 0.3 log units = good)
direction_correct: Did the model predict the correct direction of change?
reasoning_elements:
  • Identifies relevant metabolic soft spots or blocking groups
  • Discusses electronic effects (electron-donating/withdrawing)
  • Mentions lipophilicity/logP changes if relevant
  • References steric effects if relevant
  • Correctly identifies CYP-relevant structural features for CYP tasks

Expert Review

property_delta Hepatocyte Intrinsic Clearance Task 2/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a matched molecular pair for intrinsic clearance in human hepatocytes. Compound A: O=C(O)COc1ccc(Cl)cc1CN1CCCN(S(=O)(=O)Cc2ccccc2)CC1 Hepatocyte Intrinsic Clearance: 1.255 log CLint (µL/min/10^6 cells) Compound B: O=C(O)COc1ccc(Cl)cc1CN1CCCN(S(=O)(=O)c2ccccc2)CC1 Compound B differs from A by the structural transformation: c1ccc(C[*:1])cc1 → c1ccc([*:1])cc1 (Core/scaffold: O=C(O)COc1ccc(Cl)cc1CN1CCCN(S(=O)(=O)[*:1])CC1) Questions: 1. Predict the Hepatocyte Intrinsic Clearance of Compound B. 2. Will this transformation result in improved hepatocyte stability or reduced hepatocyte stability? 3. Explain the chemical/mechanistic reasoning behind your prediction. What structural or electronic features drive this change?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Analysis of the Benzylsulfonyl → Phenylsulfonyl Transformation ### Structural Change - **Compound A:** piperazine-N-**SO₂CH₂Ph** (benzylsulfonyl) - **Compound B:** piperazine-N-**SO₂Ph** (phenylsulfonyl) - The transformation removes the methylene bridge between the phenyl and the sulfonyl. --- ### 1. Predicted CLint for Compound B **~0.6–0.8 log CLint (µL/min/10⁶ cells)** — approximately 3–5× lower than Compound A. --- ### 2. Direction of Change **Improved hepatocyte stability** — Compound B will clear more slowly. --- ### 3. Mechanistic Reasoning **Primary driver: elimination of a benzylic sp³ metabolic soft spot** In Compound A, the benzylic CH₂ (PhCH₂-SO₂-) is the dominant site of CYP-mediated oxidation. CYP3A4/2C enzymes readily abstract the benzylic H via a radical mechanism to give the α-hydroxysulfone: **Ph-CH(OH)-SO₂-N**. This intermediate is chemically competent to fragment — retroelimination releases benzaldehyde and the piperazine sulfonamide (or variants thereof), contributing to high intrinsic clearance. Even if fragmentation is slow, the α-hydroxysulfone itself represents turnover. In Compound B, this entire pathway is closed: there is **no benzylic sp³ carbon**. The phenyl ring is directly bonded to sulfonyl. **Secondary driver: electronic deactivation of the aromatic ring toward CYP-mediated aromatic hydroxylation** In Compound A, the CH₂ insulates the phenyl ring electronically from the sulfonyl, leaving the ring with roughly toluene-like electron density — moderately susceptible to aromatic oxidation. In Compound B, the phenyl is directly attached to -SO₂-, one of the strongest π-withdrawing groups in medicinal chemistry (σₚ ≈ +0.68). The ring becomes highly electron-poor, strongly disfavoring the electrophilic aromatic substitution-like CYP iron-oxo mechanism for aromatic hydroxylation. The *ipso*, *ortho*, and *para* positions are particularly deactivated. **No alternative soft spots are introduced** The N-SO₂ bond and the sulfonyl S=O bonds are metabolically inert. The rest of the scaffold (acetic acid linker, chlorobenzene ether, piperazine N-CH₂) is unchanged. **Net effect:** Compound B loses its most kinetically accessible oxidation site and gains electronic protection against aromatic hydroxylation. This is a textbook metabolic hardening strategy in DMPK-driven lead optimization.

Ground Truth

value_B: 0.6990
delta: -0.5563
direction: beneficial
transform: c1ccc(C[*:1])cc1>>c1ccc([*:1])cc1
transform_population_mean_delta: 0.0400
transform_population_n_pairs: 5
transform_population_std: 0.3882

property_delta Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance Task 3/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a matched molecular pair for intrinsic clearance in human liver microsomes. Compound A: COC(c1ccc(F)cc1Br)c1cnc2n1CCN(c1cn[nH]c(=O)c1Cl)C2 Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance: 1.223 log CLint (µL/min/mg) Compound B: COC(c1ccc(F)cc1C(F)(F)F)c1cnc2n1CCN(c1cn[nH]c(=O)c1Cl)C2 Compound B differs from A by the structural transformation: Br[*:1] → FC(F)(F)[*:1] (Core/scaffold: COC(c1ccc(F)cc1[*:1])c1cnc2n1CCN(c1cn[nH]c(=O)c1Cl)C2) Questions: 1. Predict the Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance of Compound B. 2. Will this transformation result in improved metabolic stability (lower clearance) or reduced metabolic stability (higher clearance)? 3. Explain the chemical/mechanistic reasoning behind your prediction. What structural or electronic features drive this change?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Analysis: Br → CF₃ Transformation on Metabolic Stability ### 1. Predicted Clearance of Compound B **~0.75–0.90 log CLint (µL/min/mg)** — approximately a 2–3× reduction in intrinsic clearance. ### 2. Direction of Effect **Improved metabolic stability (lower clearance).** This is a reliable SAR outcome in medicinal chemistry — Br → CF₃ is routinely deployed as a metabolic-stabilizing bioisostere. ### 3. Mechanistic Reasoning **Primary metabolic soft spot: the benzylic methoxy (COC-Ar)** The dominant CYP-mediated liability in this scaffold is at the benzylic position — either O-demethylation of the methoxy group (CYP2C/CYP3A4-mediated HAT on the OCH₃) or direct oxidation of the benzylic C-H. Both pathways are sensitive to the electron density at and around that carbon. **Why CF₃ deactivates this site more than Br:** | Property | Br | CF₃ | |---|---|---| | Inductive withdrawal (–I) | Moderate (σ_I ≈ 0.45) | Strong (σ_I ≈ 0.54) | | Resonance donation (+M) | Yes — lone pair into ring | None — no lone pairs on C | | Net Hammett σ (para equivalent) | ~0.23 | ~0.54 | | Metabolic vulnerability of group itself | Possible aryl Br oxidation | CF₃ is completely inert | Bromine is a classic ambident substituent: it withdraws inductively but donates electrons back through resonance (+M), partially compensating. CF₃ has **no compensating resonance donation** — all three fluorines are strongly electronegative and withdrawing, and the C–F bonds (~130 kcal/mol) are immune to CYP oxidation. **Consequences at the benzylic carbon (ortho relationship):** - CF₃ in the ortho position inductively depolarizes the benzylic C–H bond, raising its bond dissociation energy and making HAT by the CYP oxo-ferryl species less thermodynamically favorable. - Reduced π-electron density across the ring lowers the rate of any arene oxide / epoxide intermediate formation (aromatic hydroxylation pathway). - CF₃ itself cannot be metabolized, eliminating a minor but non-negligible oxidative liability that exists with Br (aryl halides can undergo desaturation or radical pathways in some CYPs). **Steric contribution (secondary):** CF₃ is bulkier than Br in the context of a planar aromatic ring face (effective cone angle larger), which may partially occlude productive CYP active-site positioning for hydrogen abstraction from the adjacent methoxy or benzylic C-H. This is a modest but additive effect in the ortho configuration. **Lipophilicity caveat:** CF₃ raises logP by ~0.6–0.9 units relative to Br. Higher lipophilicity increases non-specific CYP binding affinity, which could partially offset the electronic stabilization. However, in this scaffold, the strong electronic deactivation at the benzylic site dominates, and the net effect is still improved stability — consistent with extensive MMP database evidence showing Br → CF₃ reduces HLM CLint in the majority of matched pairs. ### Summary CF₃ replaces Br as a stronger, non-resonance-donating electron-withdrawing group that: (1) reduces electron density at the primary metabolic soft spot (benzylic carbon), making CYP-mediated oxidation less favorable; (2) is itself completely metabolically inert, eliminating any aryl Br-related liability; and (3) provides modest steric protection. The net predicted outcome is a ~2–3× reduction in CLint (~0.3–0.5 log unit improvement).

Ground Truth

value_B: 1.7042
delta: 0.4814
direction: detrimental
transform: Br[*:1]>>FC(F)(F)[*:1]
transform_population_mean_delta: -0.0977
transform_population_n_pairs: 27
transform_population_std: 0.3657

property_delta CYP2C9 Inhibition Potency Task 4/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a matched molecular pair for CYP2C9 inhibition potency. Compound A: N#Cc1ccnc2[nH]cc(C(=O)c3c(F)ccc(NS(=O)(=O)c4cc(F)ccc4F)c3F)c12 CYP2C9 Inhibition Potency: 5.000 pIC50 Compound B: O=C(c1c(F)ccc(NS(=O)(=O)c2cc(F)ccc2F)c1F)c1c[nH]c2nccc(Cl)c12 Compound B differs from A by the structural transformation: N#C[*:1] → Cl[*:1] (Core/scaffold: O=C(c1c(F)ccc(NS(=O)(=O)c2cc(F)ccc2F)c1F)c1c[nH]c2nccc([*:1])c12) Questions: 1. Predict the CYP2C9 Inhibition Potency of Compound B. 2. Will this transformation result in reduced CYP2C9 inhibition potency or increased CYP2C9 inhibition potency? 3. Explain the chemical/mechanistic reasoning behind your prediction. What structural or electronic features drive this change?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Analysis: N#C → Cl Transformation at C-5 of the Pyrrolopyridine Core ### 1. Predicted pIC50 for Compound B: ~4.3–4.5 ### 2. Direction: **Reduced** CYP2C9 inhibition potency --- ### 3. Mechanistic Reasoning **The critical driver: loss of direct heme coordination by the nitrile** Nitrile groups are established reversible heme iron ligands in CYP enzymes. The nitrile nitrogen has a lone pair oriented linearly from the C≡N triple bond, making it geometrically ideal to coordinate Fe(III) in the CYP active site. This is analogous to the well-characterized mechanism of nitrile-containing drugs (e.g., letrozole's CYP19 inhibition, various CYP3A4 inhibitors). In Compound A, the CN at C-5 of the pyrrolopyridine likely acts as a secondary or primary heme-coordinating element, augmenting binding through a direct Fe←N≡C interaction. In Compound B, chlorine replaces this. Cl **cannot** coordinate to heme iron. Despite being isoelectronically similar in size to CN, it lacks the lone pair geometry required for metal coordination in this context. **Secondary electronic effects reinforce the prediction (or partially offset it)** | Feature | CN (Compound A) | Cl (Compound B) | |---|---|---| | Hammett σ_p | +0.66 (strong EWG) | +0.23 (weak net EWG) | | Resonance effect | π-withdrawing | π-donating (lone pairs into ring) | | Pyridine N basicity | Substantially reduced | Modestly reduced | | Heme coordination via substituent | Yes (N lone pair of CN) | No | The CN group, being a strong σ and π withdrawer, significantly reduces the electron density on the pyridine nitrogen of the azaindole — meaning the pyridine N itself is a *weaker* heme ligand in Compound A. Cl partially restores electron density via resonance donation. This alone would favor Compound B, but it cannot compensate for the complete loss of the CN→Fe coordination mode. **Lipophilicity/shape effects are minor** Cl (LogP increment ~+0.7) is slightly more lipophilic than CN (LogP increment ~−0.1), which could marginally improve passive binding in the predominantly hydrophobic CYP2C9 active site. However, this 0.8 unit cLogP increase is unlikely to overcome the loss of a direct iron coordination interaction. **Estimated magnitude:** The drop from direct nitrile heme coordination to purely passive/indirect binding typically costs 0.5–0.8 pIC50 units in matched pair analyses. I predict Compound B at **~4.3–4.5 pIC50** (IC50 ~ 30–50 µM), representing roughly a 3–5× loss of potency.

Ground Truth

value_B: 5.0000
delta: 0.0000
direction: detrimental
transform: N#C[*:1]>>Cl[*:1]
transform_population_mean_delta: 0.0381
transform_population_n_pairs: 17
transform_population_std: 0.3491

property_delta Microsomal Half-life Task 5/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a matched molecular pair for half-life in human liver microsomes. Compound A: CCOc1cc(O[C@@H]2C[C@H]3C(=O)N[C@]4(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)C5CC5)C[C@H]4/C=C\CCCCC[C@H](NC(=O)c4cc[nH]n4)C(=O)N3C2)c2ccc(OC)c(C)c2n1 Microsomal Half-life: 0.085 log t1/2 (min) Compound B: COc1ccc2c(O[C@@H]3C[C@H]4C(=O)N[C@]5(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)C6CC6)C[C@H]5/C=C\CCCCC[C@H](NC(=O)c5cc[nH]n5)C(=O)N4C3)cc(OC(C)C)nc2c1C Compound B differs from A by the structural transformation: CC[*:1] → CC(C)[*:1] (Core/scaffold: COc1ccc2c(O[C@@H]3C[C@H]4C(=O)N[C@]5(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)C6CC6)C[C@H]5/C=C\CCCCC[C@H](NC(=O)c5cc[nH]n5)C(=O)N4C3)cc(O[*:1])nc2c1C) Questions: 1. Predict the Microsomal Half-life of Compound B. 2. Will this transformation result in increased half-life or decreased half-life? 3. Explain the chemical/mechanistic reasoning behind your prediction. What structural or electronic features drive this change?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Analysis: Ar-OEt → Ar-OiPr Transformation in HLM --- ### 1. Predicted Half-life of Compound B Compound A: 0.085 log t₁/₂ → ~1.22 min **Predicted for Compound B: ~0.25–0.40 log t₁/₂ (~1.8–2.5 min)** --- ### 2. Direction: Increased Half-life The isopropoxy variant (B) is predicted to be **more metabolically stable** than the ethoxy variant (A). --- ### 3. Mechanistic Reasoning #### Primary Pathway Under Attack: O-Dealkylation For Ar–O–R ethers, the dominant CYP-mediated route is **α-carbon hydrogen abstraction → hemiacetal collapse → phenol release**. The relevant CYPs for this large, lipophilic macrocyclic scaffold are predominantly **CYP3A4** (likely) and **CYP2C9/2D6** secondarily. #### Competing Effects **Factor favoring faster metabolism of B (secondary C–H reactivity):** - The isopropoxy α-carbon is **secondary** (–O–CH(CH₃)₂); its C–H BDE is ~2–3 kcal/mol lower than the **primary** α-CH₂ of ethoxy (–O–CH₂CH₃) - Lower BDE = intrinsically more susceptible to CYP ferryl-oxo hydrogen abstraction - In isolation, this would predict *faster* O-dealkylation for the isopropoxy compound **Factor favoring slower metabolism of B (steric shielding — dominant here):** - The isopropyl group's **gem-dimethyl branching** imposes significant steric demand on the CYP active site - For productive O-dealkylation, the α-carbon must be positioned within ~4 Å of the compound I [Fe=O]³⁺ species. The two flanking methyls of isopropyl create a geometric constraint: when one methyl avoids clashing with the active site residues, the other methyl is driven into steric conflict - CYP3A4 — though large — operates on a structurally rigid macrocyclic substrate (this molecule has ~MW 800–900), limiting conformational freedom in the binding pose; steric effects on the ether side chain are amplified - This is mechanistically analogous to why *neo*-pentyl and *tert*-alkyl groups resist oxidation far more than their primary/secondary BDE would predict #### Why Steric Wins Over Intrinsic C–H Reactivity In CYP kinetics, the rate-limiting step for bulky substrates is typically **binding geometry/productive complex formation**, not the bond dissociation step per se. The binding $K_m$ is driven partly by steric fit. For a large macrocyclic substrate on CYP3A4: - The scissile C–H must be geometrically proximal to Fe=O **and** the rest of the molecule must be accommodated — leaving little tolerance for steric clashes at the ether side chain - With OEt, the primary –CH₂– presents a low-steric-profile target - With OiPr, the branching forces one methyl group toward protein residues regardless of rotameric state, reducing the frequency of productive binding poses #### Additional Electronic Consideration The isopropyl group is marginally more electron-donating than ethyl (+I effect), slightly increasing electron density on the aromatic ring via the oxygen. This modestly attenuates the ring's electrophilic aromatic hydroxylation susceptibility, a minor but directionally consistent effect. #### Caveat This molecule has multiple metabolic soft spots (macrolactam amide, the N–H acylsulfonamide warhead, the pyrazole, aliphatic chain). If O-dealkylation is **not** the dominant clearance pathway, the OEt→OiPr change may have smaller-than-expected impact on overall HLM t₁/₂. The MMP signal here is likely real but modest in magnitude (~0.15–0.3 log units improvement).

Ground Truth

value_B: 0.1903
delta: 0.1050
direction: beneficial
transform: CC[*:1]>>CC(C)[*:1]
transform_population_mean_delta: -0.0833
transform_population_n_pairs: 24
transform_population_std: 0.3161

property_delta Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance Task 6/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a matched molecular pair for intrinsic clearance in human liver microsomes. Compound A: Cc1cc(Nc2ccc(S(F)(F)(F)(F)F)cc2)n2nc(C(F)(F)F)nc2n1 Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance: 3.845 log CLint (µL/min/mg) Compound B: Cc1cc(Nc2ccc(S(F)(F)(F)(F)F)cc2)n2nc(C3CC3)nc2n1 Compound B differs from A by the structural transformation: FC(F)(F)[*:1] → C1CC1[*:1] (Core/scaffold: Cc1cc(Nc2ccc(S(F)(F)(F)(F)F)cc2)n2nc([*:1])nc2n1) Questions: 1. Predict the Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance of Compound B. 2. Will this transformation result in improved metabolic stability (lower clearance) or reduced metabolic stability (higher clearance)? 3. Explain the chemical/mechanistic reasoning behind your prediction. What structural or electronic features drive this change?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Analysis: CF3 → Cyclopropyl Transformation on Triazolo-pyrimidine Scaffold ### 1. Predicted CLint for Compound B **~4.3–4.8 log CLint (µL/min/mg)** — approximately +0.5 to +1.0 log unit increase over Compound A. --- ### 2. Direction of Change **Reduced metabolic stability (higher clearance)** upon replacing CF3 with cyclopropyl. --- ### 3. Mechanistic Reasoning **Why CF3 is metabolically silent:** CF3 carries three strong C–F bonds (~130 kcal/mol each). CYP450 enzymes cannot abstract hydrogen from CF3 (there is none), and direct oxidative attack on carbon is blocked by the high C–F BDE and the strongly electron-withdrawing inductive effect. CF3 is essentially a metabolic dead end — it contributes no new clearance pathway and simultaneously deactivates adjacent heterocyclic carbons toward electrophilic oxidation (σ_p = +0.54). **Why cyclopropyl introduces a soft spot:** Cyclopropane is uniquely susceptible to CYP-mediated oxidation via two mechanisms: - **Ring hydroxylation** at the methylene carbons (relatively accessible C–H bonds, secondary carbons). The resulting cyclopropanol spontaneously ring-opens to a 3-hydroxypropaldehyde or 3-hydroxypropyl intermediate. - **Radical ring opening:** CYP compound I can abstract the ring C–H, generating a cyclopropylcarbinyl radical, which ring-opens at ~10⁸ s⁻¹ — faster than radical rebound — trapping the ring-opened product. This is a diagnostic probe reaction for CYP radical mechanisms. CYP3A4 and CYP2C9 are the primary enzymes involved in aryl-cyclopropyl oxidation in compounds with this lipophilicity/electronic profile. **Electronic effect of the substitution:** The triazolo-pyrimidine core is electron-poor (multiple ring nitrogens), which suppresses aromatic ring oxidation. With CF3 installed, the C attached to the ring is further deactivated. Cyclopropyl, by contrast, donates electron density through Walsh orbital overlap/hyperconjugation into the adjacent C=N bond, slightly activating the attachment carbon and making the cyclopropyl ring more electron-rich and CYP-accessible. **Other metabolic sites remain unchanged:** Both compounds share the same soft spots: the *N*-arylamino group (susceptible to N-oxidation and aromatic hydroxylation) and the ring methyl (benzylic oxidation). The transformation adds one metabolic pathway rather than rerouting existing ones — hence a moderate rather than dramatic increase in CLint. **SF5 group:** Pentafluorosulfanyl is completely metabolically inert (analogous to CF3 but even more robust) and does not factor into the directional change. --- ### Summary | Feature | CF3 | Cyclopropyl | |---|---|---| | Metabolic reactivity | None (C–F bonds inert) | Active CYP substrate (ring opening) | | Electronic effect on scaffold | EWG, deactivates adjacent sites | Weak EDG via hyperconjugation | | New metabolic pathway | No | Yes (hydroxylation/ring opening) | | Predicted CLint | 3.85 (measured) | ~4.3–4.8 (predicted) | The cyclopropyl transformation is well-established in medicinal chemistry as a lipophilicity-neutral, shape-complementary CF3 bioisostere — but it sacrifices metabolic stability. The trade-off is intentional when the goal is to modulate hERG, solubility, or target affinity while accepting a clearance penalty.

Ground Truth

value_B: 0.6990
delta: -3.1461
direction: beneficial
transform: FC(F)(F)[*:1]>>C1CC1[*:1]
transform_population_mean_delta: 0.0838
transform_population_n_pairs: 39
transform_population_std: 0.7132

series_completion CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency Task 7/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a congeneric series of compounds that share the same molecular scaffold but differ in their R-group substitution. Each compound has a measured CYP3A4 inhibition potency value. Core scaffold: Cc1cnc2[nH]cc(Cc3ccc([*:1])nc3F)c2c1 Known compounds: Compound 1: COc1cc(CNc2ccc(Cc3c[nH]c4ncc(C)cc34)c(F)n2)c(F)cn1 R-group: COc1cc(CN[*:1])c(F)cn1 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 2: COc1ccc(CNc2ccc(Cc3c[nH]c4ncc(C)cc34)c(F)n2)c(OC)n1 R-group: COc1ccc(CN[*:1])c(OC)n1 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 3: COc1ccc(CNc2ccc(Cc3c[nH]c4ncc(C)cc34)c(F)n2)cn1 R-group: COc1ccc(CN[*:1])cn1 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 4: COc1ncc(F)cc1CNc1ccc(Cc2c[nH]c3ncc(C)cc23)c(F)n1 R-group: COc1ncc(F)cc1CN[*:1] CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 5: COc1ncccc1CNc1ccc(Cc2c[nH]c3ncc(C)cc23)c(F)n1 R-group: COc1ncccc1CN[*:1] CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 6: Cc1cnc2[nH]cc(Cc3ccc(NCc4ccc(C(F)(F)F)nc4)nc3F)c2c1 R-group: FC(F)(F)c1ccc(CN[*:1])cn1 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 7: Cc1cnc2[nH]cc(Cc3ccc(NCc4ccc(Cl)nc4)nc3F)c2c1 R-group: Clc1ccc(CN[*:1])cn1 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 8: Cc1cnc2[nH]cc(Cc3ccc(NCc4cccnc4F)nc3F)c2c1 R-group: Fc1ncccc1CN[*:1] CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 Compound 9: Cc1cnc2[nH]cc(Cc3ccc(NCc4ccnc(F)c4)nc3F)c2c1 R-group: Fc1cc(CN[*:1])ccn1 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.300 pIC50 New compound: Cc1cnc2[nH]cc(Cc3ccc(NCc4cnccc4C(F)(F)F)nc3F)c2c1 R-group: FC(F)(F)c1ccncc1CN[*:1] Questions: 1. Expert Review

series_completion CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency Task 8/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a congeneric series of compounds that share the same molecular scaffold but differ in their R-group substitution. Each compound has a measured CYP2C19 inhibition potency value. Core scaffold: Nc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)N(C[*:1])c2ccnn2-c2ccccc2)cc1 Known compounds: Compound 1: CC(C)CCN(c1ccnn1-c1ccccc1)S(=O)(=O)c1ccc(N)cc1 R-group: CC(C)C[*:1] CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 4.222 pIC50 Compound 2: CCCCN(c1ccnn1-c1ccccc1)S(=O)(=O)c1ccc(N)cc1 R-group: CCC[*:1] CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 4.222 pIC50 Compound 3: CCCN(c1ccnn1-c1ccccc1)S(=O)(=O)c1ccc(N)cc1 R-group: CC[*:1] CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 3.602 pIC50 Compound 4: Nc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)N(CCCO)c2ccnn2-c2ccccc2)cc1 R-group: OCC[*:1] CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 3.824 pIC50 Compound 5: Nc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)N(Cc2ccccc2)c2ccnn2-c2ccccc2)cc1 R-group: c1ccc([*:1])cc1 CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 4.347 pIC50 New compound: CCN(c1ccnn1-c1ccccc1)S(=O)(=O)c1ccc(N)cc1 R-group: C[*:1] Questions: 1. Predict the CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency of the new compound. 2. Explain your reasoning based on the SAR trends in the series. 3. How confident are you in this prediction? What factors could make it unreliable?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Prediction and SAR Analysis ### 1. Predicted Potency: ~3.0 pIC50 (range: 2.8–3.3) --- ### 2. SAR Reasoning **Defining the substituent series correctly:** The scaffold contributes a fixed N-CH₂ (methylene) to the nitrogen; the R-group variation extends from there. Mapping net N-alkyl chain lengths: | Compound | N-substituent | pIC50 | |----------|--------------|-------| | New (ethyl) | -CH₂CH₃ (2C) | ? | | Cpd 3 (propyl) | -CH₂CH₂CH₃ (3C) | 3.602 | | Cpd 2 (n-butyl) | -CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₃ (4C) | 4.222 | | Cpd 1 (isobutyl) | -CH₂CH(CH₃)₂ (4C, branched) | 4.222 | | Cpd 4 (3-hydroxypropyl) | -CH₂CH₂CH₂OH (3C + OH) | 3.824 | | Cpd 5 (benzyl) | -CH₂Ph | 4.347 | **Dominant driver: lipophilicity/hydrophobic complementarity with the CYP2C19 active site** CYP2C19's active site is a moderately large, predominantly hydrophobic cavity. The N-alkyl substituent occupies a lipophilic sub-pocket, and the SAR shows a clear chain-length/lipophilicity dependence: - **propyl → butyl**: +0.620 pIC50 — each methylene adds ~0.3–0.6 log units via CH₂ hydrophobic burial (≈ −0.7 to −1.4 kcal/mol ΔΔG binding) - **isobutyl = n-butyl**: branching is tolerated; the pocket accommodates branched chains without steric penalty, suggesting flexibility in the hydrophobic sub-pocket - **hydroxypropyl > propyl (+0.22)**: despite polarity, the terminal OH likely forms a H-bond with a polar CYP2C19 residue (candidates: Asn286, Gln212 — consistent with known CYP2C19 polar residue topology), partially compensating for desolvation cost - **benzyl > butyl (+0.125)**: the phenyl ring adds a small increment via favorable π-stacking or additional lipophilic surface area **Extrapolation to N-ethyl:** Removing one methylene from propyl to ethyl should cost approximately 0.5–0.7 pIC50 units (similar magnitude to the propyl→butyl step, possibly slightly attenuated because the incremental hydrophobic burial of the terminal methyl in propyl is smaller than that of the chain terminus in butyl). This gives: **3.602 − 0.6 ≈ 3.0 pIC50** The electronic and geometric properties of the sulfonamide nitrogen are unchanged; the aniline NH₂ anchor and the N-phenylpyrazole heme-coordinating fragment are invariant across the series, so potency variation is governed almost entirely by the N-substituent hydrophobic volume. --- ### 3. Confidence Assessment **Moderate confidence (~65%)** **Factors supporting the prediction:** - Clear, monotonic lipophilicity-driven trend across 3 linear alkyl data points - Internally consistent SAR (isobutyl = n-butyl confirms hydrophobic volume, not shape, is key) - Physico-chemical logic is sound for a CYP2C19 inhibition assay **Factors that could make it unreliable:** 1. **Non-linearity at short chain lengths**: Below propyl, a conformational threshold effect could occur if the N-ethyl group is too short to anchor the molecule into the hydrophobic sub-pocket, causing a steeper-than-expected drop (cliff effect, potentially <2.8). 2. **Sulfonamide N-geometry change**: Reducing steric bulk on nitrogen from propyl to ethyl increases N-lone pair availability and could alter the pyramidalization/rotation of the sulfonamide N. This affects the dihedral between the 4-aminophenyl and pyrazole arms, potentially disrupting the binding pose in ways not captured by a simple chain-length model. 3. **Sparse training set**: Only one directly relevant anchor (propyl = 3.602); the prediction extrapolates downward from a single point, not interpolates. 4. **Assay dynamic range**: Both butyl compounds report exactly 4.222 — this quantization suggests the assay or data curation introduces rounding. The true resolution may be ±0.3 log units. 5. **Heme coordination distance**: If N-ethyl adopts a conformation where the pyrazole nitrogen-to-iron distance shifts outside the optimal 2.0–2.3 Å coordination window (due to the lighter alkyl arm changing the N-C bond preferred conformation), potency could fall more steeply than predicted by lipophilicity alone. **Bottom line**: The compound is likely a weak CYP2C19 inhibitor (~3.0 pIC50, IC₅₀ ~1 mM), consistent with the hydrophobic desaturation trend but with meaningful uncertainty of ±0.4 log units.

Ground Truth

value: 3.6478
r_group: C[*:1]
series_values: [4.2218, 4.2218, 3.6021, 3.8239, 4.3468]
series_mean: 4.0433
series_std: 0.2824

Evaluation Criteria

prediction_accuracy: Within 0.3 log units of actual = good, within 0.5 = acceptable
sar_reasoning: Does the model identify trends in the series?
calibration: Is the stated confidence appropriate given series variance?
reasoning_elements:
  • Identifies SAR trend from the series data
  • Relates structural features of the R-group to the property
  • Considers similarity to other R-groups in the series
  • Acknowledges uncertainty appropriately

Core scaffold: CC(C)c1ccccc1[C@@H]1CCCN1C1CC2(CCN(c3ccc(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)c4ccc([*:1])c([N+](=O)[O-])c4)c(Oc4cnc5[nH]ccc5c4)c3)CC2)C1 Known compounds: Compound 1: CC(C)c1ccccc1[C@@H]1CCCN1C1CC2(CCN(c3ccc(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)c4ccc(NCC5CCOCC5)c([N+](=O)[O-])c4)c(Oc4cnc5[nH]ccc5c4)c3)CC2)C1 R-group: C1CC(CN[*:1])CCO1 CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 4.523 pIC50 Compound 2: CC(C)c1ccccc1[C@@H]1CCCN1C1CC2(CCN(c3ccc(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)c4ccc(NC[C@@H]5COCCO5)c([N+](=O)[O-])c4)c(Oc4cnc5[nH]ccc5c4)c3)CC2)C1 R-group: C1CO[C@H](CN[*:1])CO1 CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 5.000 pIC50 Compound 3: CC(C)c1ccccc1[C@@H]1CCCN1C1CC2(CCN(c3ccc(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)c4ccc(NC[C@H]5COCCO5)c([N+](=O)[O-])c4)c(Oc4cnc5[nH]ccc5c4)c3)CC2)C1 R-group: C1CO[C@@H](CN[*:1])CO1 CYP2C19 Inhibition Potency: 5.000 pIC50 New compound: CC(C)c1ccccc1[C@@H]1CCCN1C1CC2(CCN(c3ccc(C(=O)NS(=O)(=O)c4ccc(NC[C@H]5CC[C@](C)(O)CC5)c([N+](=O)[O-])c4)c(Oc4cnc5[nH]ccc5c4)c3)CC2)C1 R-group: C[C@]1(O)CC[C@H](CN[*:1])CC1 Questions: 1. Expert Review

series_completion CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency Task 10/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a congeneric series of compounds that share the same molecular scaffold but differ in their R-group substitution. Each compound has a measured CYP3A4 inhibition potency value. Core scaffold: CCCc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)Nc2ccc(F)c([*:1])c2F)cc1 Known compounds: Compound 1: CCCc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)Nc2ccc(F)c(C(=O)c3c[nH]c4ncnc(C)c34)c2F)cc1 R-group: Cc1ncnc2[nH]cc(C(=O)[*:1])c12 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.120 pIC50 Compound 2: CCCc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)Nc2ccc(F)c(C(=O)c3c[nH]c4ncnc(NC(C)C)c34)c2F)cc1 R-group: CC(C)Nc1ncnc2[nH]cc(C(=O)[*:1])c12 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.120 pIC50 Compound 3: CCCc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)Nc2ccc(F)c(C(=O)c3c[nH]c4ncnc(NC5CC5)c34)c2F)cc1 R-group: O=C(c1c[nH]c2ncnc(NC3CC3)c12)[*:1] CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.120 pIC50 Compound 4: CCCc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)Nc2ccc(F)c(C(=O)c3c[nH]c4ncnc(OC)c34)c2F)cc1 R-group: COc1ncnc2[nH]cc(C(=O)[*:1])c12 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.120 pIC50 Compound 5: CCCc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)Nc2ccc(F)c(C(=O)c3c[nH]c4ncnc(OCC)c34)c2F)cc1 R-group: CCOc1ncnc2[nH]cc(C(=O)[*:1])c12 CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency: 5.120 pIC50 New compound: CCCc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)Nc2ccc(F)c(C(=O)c3c[nH]c4ncnc(Cl)c34)c2F)cc1 R-group: O=C(c1c[nH]c2ncnc(Cl)c12)[*:1] Questions: 1. Predict the CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency of the new compound. 2. Explain your reasoning based on the SAR trends in the series. 3. How confident are you in this prediction? What factors could make it unreliable?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## CYP3A4 Inhibition Potency Prediction --- ### 1. Prediction: **5.120 pIC50** --- ### 2. SAR Reasoning **The series shows completely flat SAR across all five compounds**, despite spanning substantial chemical diversity at C4 of the 7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine: | Compound | C4 Substituent | LogP trend | HBD/HBA | pIC50 | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Me | low | 0 / 0 | 5.120 | | 2 | NHiPr | medium | 1 / 1 | 5.120 | | 3 | NHcyclopropyl | medium | 1 / 1 | 5.120 | | 4 | OMe | low-medium | 0 / 1 | 5.120 | | 5 | OEt | medium | 0 / 1 | 5.120 | The potency is invariant across: (a) a 2-carbon lipophilicity range, (b) presence or absence of H-bond donors, (c) nitrogen vs. oxygen donors, and (d) primary vs. secondary amine. This rules out any meaningful C4 contribution to CYP3A4 binding. **Structural interpretation:** The CYP3A4 inhibition is driven by the common scaffold — specifically the **n-propylbenzene sulfonamide** with the 2,4-difluoroaniline. The propyl chain and aromatic ring system likely bind in CYP3A4's large, lipophilic active site cavity (canonical hydrophobic/π-stacking interactions). The heteroaromatic R-group attached via the acyl bridge at the ortho position of the aniline most likely projects into solvent or a non-productive vector relative to the heme. **The Cl analog:** Chloro at C4 of the pyrimidine is an electronegative, moderately lipophilic substituent with no H-bond donor capacity — well within the range already sampled. It doesn't change the binding pharmacophore, and the purine ring electronics at C4 (already shown to be insensitive to Me vs. OEt vs. NHiPr) won't be meaningfully perturbed. **Prediction: 5.120 pIC50.** --- ### 3. Confidence and Reliability **Confidence: Moderate-high for the directional call, but with an important caveat.** **The major concern:** All five compounds measuring at exactly **5.120 pIC50** is suspicious — this looks like a soft assay floor/ceiling (a single-point inhibition threshold, a computational prediction bin, or a detection limit). If so, the true potency differences exist but are unresolvable in this dataset, and my prediction is really "also inactive at this threshold," which is still correct but trivially so. **Factors that could make the prediction unreliable:** 1. **Assay artifact:** If 5.120 is a clamp value, any compound below a certain activity gets assigned this number — the new compound could be meaningfully more or less potent in truth. 2. **Chloro-specific reactivity:** Chlorinated positions on electron-deficient aromatics (like a C4-chloropyrimidine) are susceptible to nucleophilic aromatic substitution. CYP3A4 has active-site cysteines and the heme-iron can generate reactive intermediates. In rare cases, chloropurines act as mechanism-based or covalent inhibitors — this could produce anomalously higher potency not predicted from the reversible inhibition SAR. 3. **Dataset size:** Five compounds is too small to have high confidence that the flat SAR is genuine rather than accidental. A sixth compound could break the trend. 4. **Metabolic activation:** The Cl substituent changes metabolic stability relative to OMe/OEt (no O-dealkylation pathway), which could alter in-cell exposure, but this doesn't affect direct CYP3A4 inhibition potency in a biochemical assay. **Bottom line:** The flat series strongly justifies 5.120 as the prediction, but the uniformity of the data itself is a flag that the assay may not be discriminating in this potency range.

Ground Truth

value: 5.1200
r_group: O=C(c1c[nH]c2ncnc(Cl)c12)[*:1]
series_values: [5.12, 5.12, 5.12, 5.12, 5.12]
series_mean: 5.1200
series_std: 0.0000

Evaluation Criteria

calibration: Is the stated confidence appropriate given series variance?

  • Considers similarity to other R-groups in the series
  • Acknowledges uncertainty appropriately

Expert Review

series_completion Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance Task 12/30

Molecules

Prompt

You are given a congeneric series of compounds that share the same molecular scaffold but differ in their R-group substitution. Each compound has a measured intrinsic clearance in human liver microsomes value. Core scaffold: CC(C)CN(S(=O)(=O)c1ccc2c(c1)C(=O)CC(C1CCOCC1)O2)[*:1] Known compounds: Compound 1: CC(C)CN(c1ccc(F)cc1)S(=O)(=O)c1ccc2c(c1)C(=O)CC(C1CCOCC1)O2 R-group: Fc1ccc([*:1])cc1 Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance: 3.246 log CLint (µL/min/mg) Compound 2: CC(C)CN(c1ccc(N(C)C)cc1)S(=O)(=O)c1ccc2c(c1)C(=O)CC(C1CCOCC1)O2 R-group: CN(C)c1ccc([*:1])cc1 Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance: 1.993 log CLint (µL/min/mg) Compound 3: Cc1nc(N2CCOCC2)ccc1N(CC(C)C)S(=O)(=O)c1ccc2c(c1)C(=O)CC(C1CCOCC1)O2 R-group: Cc1nc(N2CCOCC2)ccc1[*:1] Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance: 1.324 log CLint (µL/min/mg) New compound: CCc1ccc(N(CC(C)C)S(=O)(=O)c2ccc3c(c2)C(=O)CC(C2CCOCC2)O3)cc1 R-group: CCc1ccc([*:1])cc1 Questions: 1. Predict the Microsomal Intrinsic Clearance of the new compound. 2. Explain your reasoning based on the SAR trends in the series. 3. How confident are you in this prediction? What factors could make it unreliable?

Model Response claude-sonnet-4-6

## Prediction: ~3.4 log CLint (µL/min/mg) Range estimate: 3.2–3.7 --- ## SAR Reasoning **The dominant driver in this series is lipophilicity + metabolic soft spot count.** | Compound | R-group | cLogP contribution (π) | Metabolic vulnerability | log CLint | |---|---|---|---|---| | 3 | 2-Me-6-morpholinopyridin-3-yl | very low (heteroatoms, polar) | Moderate (morpholine N-oxide, pyridine) | 1.324 | | 2 | 4-NMe2-phenyl | low (~0.18) | N-demethylation + ring hydroxylation | 1.993 | | 1 | 4-F-phenyl | moderate (~0.14 for F) | Ring hydroxylation at unblocked positions | 3.246 | | **new** | **4-Et-phenyl** | **high (~1.02 for Et)** | **Benzylic CH₂ oxidation + ring hydroxylation** | **~3.4** | **Key mechanistic points:** 1. **Lipophilicity trend is monotonic.** Moving from morpholinopyridyl → NMe₂-phenyl → F-phenyl, cLogP increases and CLint rises by ~1.9 log units. The para-ethylphenyl substituent has a Hansch π value of ~+1.02 — roughly 7× the lipophilicity contribution of F (+0.14). This substantially increases passive diffusion into the microsomal membrane and CYP active site affinity. 2. **New metabolic soft spot: benzylic position.** The para-ethyl group introduces a benzylic CH₂ that is highly susceptible to CYP3A4/2D6-mediated hydroxylation → 4-(1-hydroxyethyl)phenyl. The terminal methyl is also subject to ω-oxidation. The F-phenyl lacks analogous aliphatic soft spots; its clearance is driven purely by aromatic hydroxylation, which is slower. 3. **The NMe₂ compound (1.993) despite having N-demethylation available.** N-demethylation is a fast CYP reaction, yet its CLint is lower than F — this is likely because the NMe₂ group is a hydrogen bond donor/acceptor that reduces membrane partitioning and increases aqueous solubility, partially counteracting the metabolic vulnerability. This confirms lipophilicity is the dominant variable. 4. **Ceiling effect consideration.** The 4-F-phenyl already sits at 3.246 log CLint. The ethyl group increases lipophilicity significantly more, but if scaffold-based clearance (lactone, isobutyl N-CH₂, THP ring) is the rate-limiting bottleneck, the R-group effect may plateau. This argues for a modest, not dramatic, increase over 3.246. --- ## Confidence Assessment **Moderate confidence.** Central estimate ~3.4, but the uncertainty interval is wide (~±0.4 log units). **What supports the prediction:** - Clear monotonic lipophilicity/CLint correlation across 3 compounds - Ethyl's metabolic liability is well-established and mechanistically predictable - Direction of change (higher than F-phenyl) is unambiguous **What could undermine it:** 1. **Only 3 training points** — you cannot reliably fit a quantitative LFER from this. The extrapolation into a more lipophilic space is a genuine extrapolation. 2. **Scaffold-dominated clearance.** If the benzoxazinone lactone ring, the THP-bearing stereocentre, or the N-isobutyl are the primary metabolic sites, R-group lipophilicity becomes less predictive. The trend in the known series could be partly coincidental. 3. **Non-linear CYP binding.** At high lipophilicity, compounds can non-specifically bind microsomal protein rather than enter the CYP active site productively — this *decreases* observed CLint (the "lipophilicity cliff"). Ethylphenyl may not be hydrophobic enough to trigger this, but it limits the upper bound. 4. **CYP isoform shifts.** Moving from F-phenyl to ethylphenyl could shift the dominant CYP from 2C9/2C19 (arene oxidation) to 3A4/2D6 (benzylic oxidation), with different kinetic constants and inhibition sensitivity. 5. **No stereochemical/conformation data.** The THP-bearing stereocentre affects sulfonamide N-geometry; R-group steric bulk (ethyl vs. F) could subtly alter the bioactive conformation presented to CYP. **Bottom line for a medicinal chemistry decision:** I would flag this compound as likely high-CLint (>3.2), in the same metabolic liability class as compound 1, and probably slightly worse. Replacing the ethyl with a CF₃ or CN group would provide a directionally better analogue in the same steric space.

Ground Truth

value: 2.4600
r_group: CCc1ccc([*:1])cc1
series_values: [3.2459, 1.9934, 1.3243]
series_mean: 2.1879
series_std: 0.7964

Evaluation Criteria

prediction_accuracy: Within 0.3 log units of actual = good, within 0.5 = acceptable
sar_reasoning: Does the model identify trends in the series?
calibration: Is the stated confidence appropriate given series variance?
reasoning_elements:
  • Identifies SAR trend from the series data
  • Relates structural features of the R-group to the property
  • Considers similarity to other R-groups in the series
  • Acknowledges uncertainty appropriately

Expert Review
